The second translation features a better mastery of language and a better representation of the original.

comment: This was a difficult decision. For some reason, I am not a big fan of either translation. Both include great as well as poor phrasing. Ultimately, the reason why I chose the second translation is because the first one contains an error. In French, "[...] Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantit脙漏 de provisions [...]" means that Hatteras HAD SOMEONE do something. He didn't do this thing himself. The translation I selected uses an avoidance strategy: "[...] a large quantity of provisions [...] were brought on deck" where the use of the passive erases agentivity (the person or thing performing the action of the verb). That works, because even though it is not a very accurate translation, it does not contradict the original meaning. The translation I did NOT select, however, did go into this contradiction with: "[...] Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions [...]". This sentence is a contradiction to the original text and it is false. It might seem like a detail, but it could potentially be of crucial importance for the story. You never know.

comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one is too literal and contains mistranslations. It picked the meaning of "settle" while the good translation picked the correct meaning of "regulate".

translation_a: Emma did not answer. She was breathing hard and staring wildly all around, while the countrywoman, frightened by her face, was instinctively backing away, thinking she was mad. Suddenly she struck her forehead and cried out, for the memory of Rodolphe, like a great bolt of lightning on a dark night, had entered her mind. He was so good, so sensitive, so generous! And besides, if he should hesitate to do her this service, she would know quite well how to force him by reminding him, with a single glance, of their lost love. She therefore set off toward La Huchette, quite unaware that she was hastening to yield to the very thing that had, only recently, so enraged her, nor in the least suspecting that she was prostituting herself.
translation_b: Emma didn't answer. She was panting, rolling her eyes around her, while the peasant woman, frightened by her face, instinctively recoiled, thinking her mad. And, moreover, if he hesitated to do her this favor, she would know how to compel him to do so by recalling their lost love with a single wink. So she set off for La Huchette, without noticing that she was running to offer herself to what had just so exasperated her, nor suspecting the least in the world of this prostitution.

translation_a: We were in the first days of October. There was fog over the countryside. Vapors stretched out on the horizon, between the outline of the hills; and others, tearing themselves apart, mounted, were lost. Sometimes, in the parting of the clouds, under a ray of sunshine, one could see in the distance the roofs of Yonville, with the gardens at the edge of the water, the courtyards, the walls, and the steeple of the church. translation_a: As a soccer player Volodya represented a complete opposite to Getzke. Volodya was a professional sportsman芒聙聯the other was a professional player. Important to Volodya was the general course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome芒聙聯Getzke strove only to show his own skill. He was an old, experienced player, not intending to maintain the honor of the team: he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sort of sports organization because he had compromised himself by moves from club to club for money. They forbade him to participate in matches for the play-off of a championship. They invited him only for friendly games, demonstration matches and trips to other countries. Skill united in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He was suspicious of the players芒聙聯both those with whom he played and the opponents. He knew that he would drive in the balls on any team. The rest was not important to him. He was a hack.
translation_b: As a football player, Volodya was the exact opposite of Getska. Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player. Volodya was interested in the overall course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome - Getsuke sought only to show his art. He was an old, experienced player who did not intend to uphold the honor of the team; he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sports organization, because he compromised himself by moving from club to club for money. He was banned from participating in championship matches. He was invited only to a friendly game, exhibition matches and trips to other countries. Art was combined in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents. He knew that he would score goals for any team. The rest didn't matter to him. He was a hack.
comment: There is no doubt that the first translation is better than the second one for the following reasons: 1) There is a mistake in spelling the name of the character in the second translation. It should be "Getzke" while the author of the second translation writes "Getska" or even "Getsuke". 2) According to the source text a story is told about two characters. The second sentence of the passage should give information about two different people while there is only one person mentioned in the second sentence of the 2nd translation: "Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player" 3) The author of the second translation uses a word-by-word translation of the sentence "He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents" which cannot be used here

translation_a: The Christian teaching given to me in childhood disappeared in me as in others, only with the difference that as I began to read a great deal and to think very early on, so my rejection of Christian teaching became a conscious one very early on. From the age of sixteen I stopped saying my prayers and of my own volition stopped going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in all that had been given to me since childhood, but I did believe in something. What I did believe in I couldn芒聙聶t possibly have said. I did believe in God or rather did not deny God, but I couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god; I didn芒聙聶t deny Christ and his teaching, but I also couldn芒聙聶t have said where his teaching lay.
translation_b: The doctrine communicated to me from childhood disappeared in me just as it did in others, with the only difference being that since I began to read and think a lot very early, my renunciation of the doctrine became conscious very early. From the age of sixteen, I stopped standing up for prayer and stopped, on my own impulse, going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in what I was told from childhood, but I believed in something. What I believed in, I could never say. I also believed in God, or rather I did not deny God, but which God I could not say; I did not deny Christ and his teaching, but what his teaching was, I could not say either.
comment: I consider the first translation to be more accurate when it comes to certain details: 1) The source text makes it clear that the narrator tells about Christian teaching, that is why I don't see any reason to call it just "doctrine" as in the second translation 2) The phrase "of my own volition" in the first translation conveys the meaning of the source sentence much better than "on my own impulse" in the second translation 3) According to the source text the narrator "couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god" he believed, that is why the translation "which God I could not say" in the second translation is inappropriate 4) If we consider the context of the source text I believe it's better to use Past Perfect in the sentences that refer to the prior actions in the past. Past Perfect is used in the first translation

translation_a: This unexpected blow nearly killed my father. He lost his usual firmness, and his grief (usually mute) poured out in bitter complaints. "How!" he repeated, losing his temper. - 芒聙聹My son participated in the plans of Pugachev! Good God, what have I come to! The empress saves him from execution! Does that make it easier for me? The execution is not terrible: my ancestor died at the place of execution, defending what he considered the shrine of his conscience; my father suffered along with Volynsky and Khrushchev. But the nobleman should change his oath, unite with robbers, murderers, runaway lackeys!.. Shame and disgrace to our family! human opinion. My father was inconsolable.
translation_b: This unexpected blow very nearly killed my father. He lost his habitual self-control, and his grief, usually silent, found expression in bitter complaints. 芒聙聹What!芒聙?he repeated, beside himself. 芒聙聹My son is an accomplice of Pugachov芒聙聶s! Merciful heavens, what have I lived to see! The Empress reprieves him! Does that make it any better for me? It芒聙聶s not the death penalty that is terrible. My great-grandfather died on the scaffold for what was to him a matter of conscience; my father suffered, together with Volynsky and Khrushchov.a But for a gentleman to betray his oath of allegiance and join brigands, murderers and runaway serfs! Shame and disgrace to our name!芒聙?Terrified by his despair, my mother did not dare to weep in his presence and tried to cheer him by talking of the uncertainty of rumor and the small faith to be attached to people芒聙聶s opinions. comment: The second translation provides a more accurate translation of the phrases from the source text that cannot be translated word by word. The author of the second translation has managed to perfectly convey the meaning of the sentences "But for a gentleman to betray his oath of allegiance and join brigands, murderers and runaway serfs" (which is translated in a wrong way in the first translation and thus failing to convey the meaning of the Russian sentence) and "Terrified by his despair, my mother did not dare to weep in his presence and tried to cheer him by talking of the uncertainty of rumor and the small faith to be attached to people芒聙聶s opinions" (which is missing in the first translation).

translation_a: In the evening he sits at home blanketed by the palm tree green of a lamp shade. Before him are sheets of paper, notebooks, and little slips with columns of figures. He throws back pages of the desk calendar, jumps up, checks the book rack, takes out some packets, and with his belly on the table, supporting his fat face with his hands, reads. The green field of the table is covered with a sheet of glass. So what is so special about that? A man is working, a man is at home in the evening working. A man staring at a piece of paper is twisting a pencil in his ear. Nothing special. But his whole behavior says: you are a Philistine, Kavalerov. Of course, he doesn芒聙聶t state this. There musn芒聙聶t even be anything of the sort on his mind. But it芒聙聶s clear without words. Some third person announces it to me. Some third person forces me to fly into a fit at the very time I芒聙聶m watching him.
translation_b: In the evening, at home, he sits, shaded by the palm greens of the lampshade. In front of him are sheets of paper, notebooks, small sheets of paper with columns of numbers. He flips the pages of the desk calendar, jumps up, looks in the bookcase, takes out packs, kneels on a chair and, with his stomach on the table, propping his fat face in his hands, reads. The green area of the table is covered with a glass plate. After all, what is special? A man works, a man is at home, in the evening, he works. The man, staring at the sheet, picks his ear with a pencil. Nothing special. But all his behavior says: you are an inhabitant, Kavalerov. Of course, he does not state this. There must be nothing like it in his mind. But it is clear without words. Someone else tells me about it. Someone else makes me mad while I'm watching him.
comment: Both translations are rather good when it comes to conveying the style of the source text. (even though "Philistine" from the first translation is a better equivalent to the Russian term "脨戮脨卤脩聥脨虏脨掳脩聜脨碌脨禄脩聦"), that is why I paid attention to the grammar and vocabulary. The first translation is way better. Speaking of vocabulary, the phrases in the first translation ("Philistine", "third person forces me to fly into a fit") are more accurate and convey the exact meaning of the Russian sentences

translation_a: But Ganya had already endured too much that day and that evening, and was not prepared for this last unexpected trial. The crowd parted into two halves before him, and he was left face to face with Nastasya Filippovna, three steps away from her. She stood right by the fireplace and waited, not tearing her burning, intent gaze from him. Ganya, in a tailcoat, his hat and gloves in his hand, stood silent and unresponding before her, his arms crossed, looking at the fire. An insane smile wandered over his face, which was pale as a sheet. True, he could not take his eyes off the fire, off the smoldering packet; but it seemed something new had arisen in his soul; it was as if he had sworn to endure the torture; he did not budge from the spot; in a few moments it became clear to everyone that he would not go after the packet, that he did not want to.
translation_b: But Ganya had already endured too much that day and that evening, and he was not prepared for this last unexpected test. The crowd parted before them into two halves, and he remained eye to eye with Nastasya Filippovna, three paces away from her. She stood right by the fireplace and waited, not taking her eyes off him, fiery, intently. Ganya, in a tailcoat, with a hat in his hand and with gloves, stood in front of her silently and without answer, arms folded and looking at the fire. An insane smile roamed across his face as pale as a handkerchief. True, he could not take his eyes off the fire, from the smoldering pack; but it seemed that something new had entered his soul; as if he had sworn to endure torture; he didn't move; after a few moments it became clear to everyone that he would not go for a pack, he did not want to go.
comment: The grammar and style in both translations are just fine. That is why I prefer the first translation due to its vocabulary. Such words as "trial", "three steps away from her", "pale as a sheet" are the exact accurate equivalents to the ones used in the source text. There is still one tiny mistake in the first translation: "The crowd parted into two halves before him" should be replaced with "The crowd parted into two halves before them" and then the whole translation will be 100% perfect

translation_a: 芒聙聹And what then? I saw gracious, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who was gentle and heard out a boy like me; I see people capable of understanding and forgiveness, people who are Russian and kind, people almost as kind and cordial as I met there, almost no worse. You can judge how joyfully surprised I was! Oh, allow me to speak this out! I had heard a lot and believed very much myself that in society everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, while the essence is exhausted; but I can see for myself now that among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us. Can it be that you are all now Jesuits and swindlers? I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight: wasn芒聙聶t it all artless, inspired humor, wasn芒聙聶t it genuinely good-natured? Can such words come from the lips of a 芒聙?dead man, with a dried-up heart and talent? Could dead people have treated me the way you have treated me?
translation_b: - So what? I saw graceful, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who caresses and listens to the boy, like me; I see people who are able to understand and forgive, Russian and kind people, almost as kind and warm-hearted as I met there, almost no worse. Judge how joyfully I was surprised! Oh, let me say it! I heard a lot and I myself very much believed that in the world everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, and the essence has dried up; but I myself now see that this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here. Are you all now Jesuits and deceivers? I heard Prince N. say just now: Isn't this simple-hearted, inspired humor, isn't this true good nature? Can such words come out of the mouth of a man... dead, with a withered heart and talent? Could the dead have treated me like you did?
comment: The first translation is better. Speaking of grammar Past Perfect should be used as in the first translation: "I had heard a lot and believed very much...". The first translation conveys the meaning properly by using accurate vocabulary: "among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us" over "this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here", "I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight" is way better than "I heard Prince N. say just now", "lips" should be used as a better equivalent to the Russian word, but not "mouth" (the second translation)
